message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The Angiotensin system is implicated in the pathogenesis of COVID19. First, ACE2 is the cellular receptor for SARS-COv-2, and expression of the
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    gene could regulate the individual’s susceptibility to infection. In addition, the balance between ACE1 and ACE activity has been implicated in the pathogenesis of respiratory diseases and could play a role in the severity of COVID19. Functional
                    <jats:italic>ACE1</jats:italic>
                    /
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    gene polymorphisms have been associated with the risk of cardiovascular and pulmonary diseases, and could thus also contribute to the outcome of COVID19.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We studied 204 COVID19 patients (137 non-severe and 67severe-ICU cases) and 536 age-matched controls. The
                    <jats:italic>ACE1</jats:italic>
                    insertion/deletion and
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    rs2285666 polymorphism were determined. Variables frequencies were compared between the groups by logistic regression. We also sequenced the ACE2 coding nucleotides in a group of patients.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Severe COVID19 was associated with hypertension male gender (p<0.001), hypertension (p=0.006), hypercholesterolaemia (p=0.046), and the ACE1-DD genotype (p=0.049). In the multiple logistic regression hypertension (p=0.02, OR=2.26, 95%CI=1.12-4.63) and male gender (p=0.002; OR=3.15, 95%CI=1.56-6.66) remained as independent significant predictors of severity. The
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    polymorphism was not associated with the disease outcome. The
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    sequencing showed no coding sequence variants that could explain an increased risk of developing COVID19.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Adverse outcome of COVID19 was associated with male gender, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia and the
                    <jats:italic>ACE1</jats:italic>
                    genotype. The
                    <jats:italic>ACE1</jats:italic>
                    -I/D was a significant risk factor for severe COVID19, but the effect was dependent on the hypertensive status.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
